confotur-dominican-republic-tax-incentiv.title: CONFOTUR Explained: The Tax Break Dominican Republic STR Investors Are Missing
confotur-dominican-republic-tax-incentiv.excerpt: CONFOTUR can exempt a Dominican Republic short-term rental from property tax, transfer tax, and import duties for up to 15 years. Here's how it actually works and who qualifies.
confotur-dominican-republic-tax-incentiv.author: Caribbean STR
confotur-dominican-republic-tax-incentiv.meta_description: A plain-English guide to CONFOTUR, the Dominican Republic's tourism tax incentive law, and how it affects short-term rental investment returns.

body.text: If you're evaluating a short-term rental purchase in the Dominican Republic, CONFOTUR is one of the most consequential pieces of fine print you'll encounter — and one of the most misunderstood. It's a tax incentive law that can materially change your investment math, but the details matter, and the marketing materials from developers don't always tell the full story.
What CONFOTUR actually is

CONFOTUR (Law 158-01, as amended) is the Dominican Republic's tourism development incentive law. Properties located within designated tourism poles — which include most of the country's major STR markets like Punta Cana, Puerto Plata, and Samana — can qualify for significant tax exemptions if the project is approved before construction begins.

For a qualifying property, the exemptions typically include relief from property tax (IPI), transfer tax on the initial purchase, and import duties on construction materials and equipment. The exemption period is commonly granted for up to 15 years from the date of approval, though the exact term depends on the specific project's CONFOTUR approval.
Why this matters for your underwriting

Property tax in the Dominican Republic (IPI) is assessed at 1% annually on the portion of a property's value exceeding a government-set threshold. For a $400,000-plus vacation property, that's a real ongoing expense — one that disappears entirely under an active CONFOTUR exemption. When you're comparing two similarly priced units, one with CONFOTUR status and one without, the tax savings alone can shift your cash-on-cash return by a meaningful margin over the holding period.

The transfer tax exemption matters at closing specifically. Dominican Republic property transfer tax is typically 3% of the registered value, so on a $400,000 purchase that's roughly $12,000 in upfront savings if the property carries CONFOTUR status at the time of sale.
What to verify before you rely on it

The single biggest mistake buyers make is assuming CONFOTUR status transfers automatically or applies to the whole development just because a sales brochure mentions it. In practice, you want documentation showing the specific decree number and approval date for the project, confirmation the exemption is still active and not nearing expiration, and written confirmation from your attorney that the exemption applies to your specific unit, not just commercial or common areas of a larger development.

It's also worth noting CONFOTUR exemptions are tied to the project's original approval, not renewed indefinitely. A development approved in 2012 with a 15-year exemption is approaching the end of its tax-free window now — which matters enormously if you're underwriting a 10-year hold.
Bottom line

CONFOTUR can be a genuine, material advantage for Dominican Republic STR investment — but it requires verification, not assumption. Every Caribbean STR report for a Dominican Republic property flags CONFOTUR status as part of the tax and legal section, specifically so this doesn't get missed during underwriting.

aruba-vs-dominican-republic-str-investme.title: Aruba vs. Dominican Republic: Where Should You Buy an STR in 2026?
aruba-vs-dominican-republic-str-investme.excerpt: Both are Caribbean STR powerhouses, but they reward very different investment strategies. Here's how the two markets actually compare on revenue, regulation, and risk.
aruba-vs-dominican-republic-str-investme.author: Caribbean STR
aruba-vs-dominican-republic-str-investme.meta_description: A side-by-side comparison of Aruba and Dominican Republic short-term rental markets for 2026, covering revenue, occupancy, regulation, and investment risk.

body.text: Aruba and the Dominican Republic are consistently the two most-searched Caribbean markets for short-term rental investment, and for good reason — both have strong, established tourism economies. But they're fundamentally different investments, and the right choice depends heavily on what kind of investor you are.
Revenue and occupancy patterns

Aruba's appeal is consistency. It sits outside the Atlantic hurricane belt, which gives it a near year-round occupancy curve without the steep seasonal dip many Caribbean markets see in late summer and early fall. That stability tends to produce more predictable cash flow projections, which matters if you're financing the purchase and need dependable debt-service coverage.

The Dominican Republic, particularly Punta Cana, runs at a larger scale with a more pronounced seasonal curve — strong winter high season, a softer shoulder period. Gross revenue potential can be very strong in absolute terms given the volume of tourism the country attracts, but the swings between peak and off-season months are wider, which means your downside-scenario modeling needs to do more work.
Regulatory and tax environment

This is where the two markets diverge most. The Dominican Republic offers CONFOTUR, the tourism tax incentive law that can exempt qualifying properties from property tax, transfer tax, and import duties for years at a time — a significant lever if your unit qualifies.

Aruba doesn't have an equivalent broad incentive program, but it offsets this with a more streamlined, foreign-investor-friendly purchase process and straightforward property tax treatment. There's less complexity to navigate, which some investors prefer even if the headline tax savings are smaller.
Buy-in cost and entry point

Dominican Republic entry pricing tends to be more accessible at the lower end of the market, with a wide range of condo product available across price points in Punta Cana and Puerto Plata. Aruba's market is smaller and more constrained by limited land, which generally pushes entry prices higher but also tends to support steadier appreciation given the supply constraint.
Which one fits your strategy

If you're prioritizing predictable, lower-volatility cash flow and a simpler regulatory path, Aruba tends to fit that profile. If you're optimizing for revenue scale and are willing to do the work of verifying CONFOTUR eligibility and modeling a more seasonal revenue curve, the Dominican Republic can offer a stronger top-line return.

Either way, the deciding factor shouldn't be a generic market reputation — it should be the specific numbers for the specific property. That's true of any market comparison, which is exactly why a property-level underwriting report matters more than a market-level overview when you're close to making an offer.
